paper.title: Scaling Long-Horizon LLM Agent via Context-Folding

blocks.summary: Context-Folding lets agents branch into subtasks and fold completed sub-trajectories into concise return summaries; FoldGRPO trains this behavior with process rewards.
Long-horizon agents suffer from performance degradation and efficiency costs when all tool calls and observations linearly accumulate in one context.
The proposed Folding Agent matches or outperforms ReAct baselines with 10x smaller active context on Deep Research and SWE benchmarks.
Vanilla ReAct models each action conditioned on the full previous trajectory, causing context growth.
Two tools implement context management: branch(description,prompt) and return(message); F folds action-observation segments between them.
Inference uses KV-cache rollback to the branch position when a return is called, making folding efficient.
The implementation is plan-execution: main thread plans and delegates; execution branches handle token-heavy subtasks without nested branches.
FoldGRPO samples grouped folded trajectories, optimizes LLM tokens under folded context F, and adds token-level process reward to outcome reward.
Process rewards include unfolded token penalty, out-of-scope penalty judged from branch prompt and return message, and failed-tool-call penalty.
Experiments cover BrowseComp-Plus for deep research and SWE-Bench Verified for agentic software engineering using Seed-OSS-36B-Instruct.
FoldGRPO reaches 0.620 Pass@1 on BrowseComp-Plus and 0.580 on SWE-Bench Verified with 32K active context and up to 10 branches.
RL increases tool calls, branches, response tokens and searched pages; FoldGRPO improves finish rate and scope accuracy while shortening main trajectory.
Folding performance improves with available branches and shows stronger advantage as combined-question complexity increases; it generalizes beyond trained branch count.
A case compresses 107K full context to 6K folded context across 4 branches.
Parallel branching obtains 0.6133 Pass@1 on BrowseComp-Plus, similar to single-branch, suggesting task structure matters.
Future work includes multi-layer context folding for hierarchical compression.
